topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a relentless, irritating tune. Sarah stared at the spreadsheet, her knuckles white where she gripped the mouse. Every number seemed to mock her, a relentless tide pulling her under. She had spent the last hour meticulously cross-referencing quarterly reports, trying to find an error, anything to justify the constant knot tightening in her chest. Then she saw it: a line item for Mark, the new analyst, showing a salary figure that dwarfed hers by a significant margin. A cold, hollow feeling washed over her, quickly followed by a sharp, almost painful intake of breath. She blinked rapidly, trying to pull the reality back into focus, but the disparity remained glaringly obvious on the screen. Her jaw tightened, and she pushed the chair back slightly, the scrape of the wheels against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet cubicle farm.

[story 2]

"Just one more thing," the manager chirped, oblivious to the internal storm brewing within David. David felt a pressure building behind his eyes, a tight band squeezing his temples. He had been tasked with compiling the departmental budget, a monumental, soul-crushing task. When he opened the compensation breakdown, the figures swam before his eyes. Mark’s entry was astronomical. David’s breath hitched, shallow and quick. He swallowed hard, the dryness in his throat making the motion difficult. He forced a neutral expression, nodding vaguely when the manager asked a question about projected spending, but inside, a frantic internal monologue was screaming about unfairness and inadequacy. He fiddled obsessively with the edge of his keyboard, his fingers trembling slightly as he tried to steady himself against the sudden, overwhelming weight of the situation.

[story 3]

Liam reread the performance review summary for the third time, his gaze snagging on the salary notations. A sudden, dizzying wave of nausea rolled through him. He tried to focus on the beige partition wall, willing the sensation to dissipate. The numbers were undeniable, stark and unforgiving. He felt a strange, hot flush creeping up his neck, and he instinctively rubbed his forehead with the heel of his hand, a futile attempt to physically ward off the sudden internal agitation. He wanted to look away, to simply disappear, but his eyes were locked on the screen. Every small sound—a phone ringing, a colleague laughing—felt amplified, grating against his already frayed nerves. He stood up abruptly, needing air, his movements jerky and uncoordinated.
